question: Define Software Engineering and User Experience. Explain how their goals differ in software development.
expectedPoints: Software Engineering: disciplined approach to designing, developing, testing, maintaining software - focuses on technical aspects
User Experience: overall experience of user interaction - focuses on satisfaction, accessibility, ease of use
SE goals: code quality, efficiency, maintainability, technical correctness
UX goals: user satisfaction, intuitive design, emotional impact, accessibility

question: What is User-Centered Design (UCD)? List any four principles of UCD approach.
expectedPoints: UCD is an approach prioritizing user needs throughout design process
Four principles: Understand user needs and contexts, Involve users in design and evaluation, Iterate design process, Consider entire user experience not just interface
Focus on user requirements before technical requirements
Validate designs through user testing

question: Explain the iterative design process. How does it help in bridging SE and UX?
expectedPoints: Iterative design involves cycles of prototyping, testing, analyzing, refining
Each iteration incorporates user feedback to improve design gradually
Helps bridge SE and UX by allowing continuous collaboration
Early prototypes can be evaluated from both technical and user perspectives
Reduces risk of expensive redesigns late in development

question: List and explain any five pain points in SE-UX collaboration.
expectedPoints: Different priorities: SE focuses on functionality and technical quality, UX on user satisfaction
Communication gaps: Different terminologies and backgrounds create misunderstandings
Late UX consideration: UX added after technical decisions made, leading to redesigns
Lack of shared vocabulary: Teams don't understand each other's constraints
Budget and time constraints: Organizations underestimate UX work needed
Difficulty quantifying UX benefits: Hard to measure ROI of UX improvements

question: What are usability heuristics? Explain any four of Nielsen's usability heuristics.
expectedPoints: Usability heuristics are general principles for interaction design
Visibility of system status: Keep users informed of what's happening
Match between system and real world: Use language familiar to users
User control and freedom: Allow easy undo/redo of actions
Consistency and standards: Maintain consistency within app and with other apps
Error prevention: Design to prevent errors before they occur

question: Explain the concept of prototyping in UX. What are the different types of prototypes?
expectedPoints: Prototyping is creating preliminary versions to test design concepts
Types: Paper prototypes (sketches on paper), Wireframes (layout guides), Mockups (static visual designs)
Interactive prototypes (clickable, functional)
Low-fidelity (simple, basic) vs High-fidelity (detailed, interactive)
Purpose: Validate with users before expensive development

question: What is Design Thinking? Describe its five phases with relevance to SE-UX integration.
expectedPoints: Design Thinking is human-centered problem-solving approach
Five phases: Empathize (understand users through research)
Define (identify and articulate the problem)
Ideate (generate creative solutions)
Prototype (build representations)
Test (validate with users)
Integrates SE and UX by requiring both technical feasibility and user desirability

question: Discuss the challenges of connecting Software Engineering and User Experience in modern software development. Suggest strategies to improve SE-UX collaboration.
expectedPoints: Challenges: Different mindsets (technical correctness vs user satisfaction), different metrics of success, communication gaps, late UX involvement, budget constraints, difficulty quantifying UX value

Strategies: Adopt agile with embedded design teams, create shared vocabulary, involve UX from project start, implement continuous user testing, use agile user stories with UX criteria, create cross-functional teams, use design systems for consistency, measure both technical and UX metrics
Benefits: Better products, reduced rework, higher user satisfaction, faster development

question: Explain how Agile methodology facilitates better integration between SE and UX. What are the potential challenges and how can they be overcome?
expectedPoints: Agile enables continuous collaboration between SE and UX through iterative sprints
UX work happens in each sprint alongside development
Regular demos allow continuous user feedback
Challenges: Time pressure, need for quick decisions, maintaining design consistency
Overcoming: Embed UX in team, create design system, conduct rapid prototyping, prioritize backlog items together, maintain constant communication
Benefits: Faster feedback, adaptability, user-centered products

question: What is User-Centered Design? Explain how UCD principles can be applied throughout the software development lifecycle to ensure better UX while maintaining technical excellence.
expectedPoints: UCD: Approach prioritizing user needs throughout design process
Application: Requirements phase - conduct user research, create personas
Design phase - apply usability heuristics, create prototypes
Development phase - implement accessible, consistent interfaces
Testing phase - conduct usability testing, gather feedback
Maintenance - continuous improvement based on user feedback
Balancing UX with technical needs through iterative testing and collaboration

question: Describe the role of prototyping in bridging the gap between Software Engineering and User Experience. Discuss various prototyping techniques and their suitability at different stages.
expectedPoints: Prototypes serve as common ground for SE and UX discussions
Low-fidelity (paper, wireframes): Quick, cheap, early exploration
High-fidelity (interactive): Closer to final product, detailed testing
Role: Validates UX decisions with users, reveals technical constraints early
Reduces expensive redesigns later in development
Facilitates communication between designers and developers
Techniques: Storyboarding, Wizard of Oz, clickable wireframes, coded prototypes
